message:
Let's clear up one of the most common myths I hear in the clinic: "doggie breath" is normal. It isn't. 🐶

That strong odor is usually one of the earliest signs of dental disease — and it's far more common than most owners realize. By age three, the majority of dogs already have some degree of periodontal disease. Left unchecked, the bacteria building up under the gumline don't just stay in the mouth; over time they can place real strain on the heart, liver, and kidneys. The early warning signs are easy to spot once you know them: bad breath, yellow-brown tartar along the gumline, and red or puffy gums.

The good news is that prevention is simple and powerful. Brushing your dog's teeth — even a few times a week with a dog-safe toothpaste — is the single most effective thing you can do at home, alongside regular checkups and professional cleanings when your vet recommends them. 🦷

When was your dog's last dental check? If you can't quite remember, it might be time. 💛
